title: Hauora o te Tinana, Hauora o te Wai: Body Wellness, Water Wellness / Traditional Healing Practices of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: This lesson is deeply connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway that flows through our rohe. Students will explore how Ngāti Kahungunu ancestors used rongoā Māori (traditional healing) practices connected to this awa, including the use of native plants like kawakawa, mānuka, and harakeke that grow along its banks. The lesson incorporates the pūrākau (traditional story) of Tāne Mahuta separating Ranginui and Papatūānuku, emphasizing the connection between environmental wellbeing and human health.
learningObjectives: Identify and describe at least three traditional rongoā Māori practices connected to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and explain their health benefits
Create a simple rongoā preparation (e.g., kawakawa balm or harakeke cordial) following traditional methods and safety protocols
Analyze how the mauri (life force) of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku connects to personal hauora using the whare tapa whā model
Collaborate in small groups to develop a protective karakia or waiata for the awa that promotes environmental and community wellbeing

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga: Connecting to Our Awa
activities.description: Students begin with a guided walk to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, where they practice karakia whakawātea (clearing prayer) and learn proper tikanga for engaging with the waterway. They identify and harvest (with permission) small samples of healing plants like kawakawa leaves, following sustainable practices taught by local kaumātua.

activities.title: Rongoā Rua: Traditional Healing Workshop
activities.description: Working with a local tohunga rongoā (healing expert), students learn to prepare traditional remedies using plants from the awa. They create kawakawa balm for skin healing and brew kawakawa tea for digestive health, documenting the process and cultural significance of each preparation.

activities.title: Whare Tapa Whā o Te Awa: Holistic Health Analysis
activities.description: Using the whare tapa whā model (taha tinana/physical, taha hinengaro/mental, taha whānau/social, taha wairua/spiritual), students analyze how the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku affects each dimension of their own hauora. They create visual representations showing these connections.

activities.title: Waiata Hauora: Healing Song Creation
activities.description: In small groups, students compose original waiata or karakia that express their connection to the awa and its healing properties. These compositions must incorporate specific references to local plants, water quality, and cultural practices, and are performed for the class.

resources: Arapeta Hāwea (local Ngāti Kahungunu tohunga rongoā available for workshops)
'Rongoā Māori: A Practical Guide to Traditional Māori Medicine' by Rob McGowan
Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku Water Quality Monitoring Kit from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
Digital archive of Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau from MTG Hawke's Bay

tasks: Create and present a digital storyboard showing the journey of a healing plant from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to its use as rongoā
Write a reflective journal entry connecting the health of the awa to personal wellbeing using the whare tapa whā model
Demonstrate proper tikanga and technique in preparing one traditional rongoā remedy

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: A3 - Safety management; B1 - Personal health and physical development; C1 - Relationships with other people; D1 - Healthy communities and environments
Social Sciences: Place and Environment - Understand how people interact with natural and cultural environments
Te Reo Māori: Whakarongo - Listening and responding to simple karakia and waiata
